prime_number.name: Prime Number
prime_number.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I belong to the world of numbers.
I always have exactly two positive divisors.
Cryptography depends heavily on me.
The smallest member of my family is 2.
My opposite is Composite Number.

educationalInfo.definition: A whole number greater than 1 with exactly two positive divisors: 1 and itself.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing prime numbers with odd numbers (e.g. 9 or 15 are odd but not prime). Believing 1 is a prime number.
educationalInfo.funFact: The number 2 is the only even prime number. All other prime numbers are odd!
practiceQuestions: Is 29 a prime number?
What is the next prime number after 19?

composite_number.name: Composite Number
composite_number.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I am a natural number, but not prime.
I have more than two positive divisors.
I can always be broken down into smaller prime factors.
Every whole number greater than 1 is either me or a prime number.
The smallest example of me is 4.

educationalInfo.definition: A positive integer that has at least one positive divisor other than 1 and itself.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking 1 is composite — it is neither prime nor composite.
educationalInfo.funFact: Every composite number can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers, known as its prime factorisation.
practiceQuestions: List all composite numbers below 20.
Why is 1 neither prime nor composite?

factors.name: Factors
factors.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I am a relationship between two numbers.
I divide a given number exactly without leaving any remainder.
The number 1 is a universal member of my group.
My count for any number is always finite.
For 12, my members are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12.

educationalInfo.definition: Numbers you multiply together to get another number, or numbers that divide a target number exactly.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing factors with multiples (factors are always less than or equal to the target number).
educationalInfo.funFact: Perfect numbers are numbers equal to the sum of their proper factors (e.g., 6 = 1 + 2 + 3).
practiceQuestions: Find all the factors of 18.
What is the greatest factor of 48?

multiples.name: Multiples
multiples.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I represent numbers that grow outward.
I am generated by multiplying a number by integers.
Unlike factors, my count for any number is infinite.
I am always greater than or equal to the original number.
For 5, my sequence starts with 5, 10, 15, 20...

educationalInfo.definition: The product of a given number and any integer.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing factors with multiples (multiples are infinite and grow larger).
educationalInfo.funFact: Zero is technically a multiple of every number, but we usually ignore it in math practice games.
practiceQuestions: What is the 5th multiple of 9?
Is 45 a multiple of 6?

lcm.name: LCM
lcm.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I help add fractions with different denominators.
I am the smallest positive multiple shared by two or more numbers.
For prime numbers a and b, I am simply equal to their product: a * b.
You can find me by listing multiples or using prime factorization.
For 4 and 6, I am 12.

educationalInfo.definition: Lowest Common Multiple: the smallest positive integer that is divisible by all numbers in a given set.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Listing factors instead of multiples when looking for the LCM, resulting in a number that is too small.
educationalInfo.funFact: The LCM of any two consecutive numbers is always their product!
practiceQuestions: Find the LCM of 6 and 8.
What is the LCM of 5 and 7?

hcf.name: HCF
hcf.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I am also known as the Greatest Common Divisor.
I am the largest positive integer that divides two or more numbers without a remainder.
For coprime numbers, I am always equal to 1.
Euclid created a famous recursive algorithm to compute me.
For 12 and 18, I am 6.

educationalInfo.definition: Highest Common Factor: the greatest common divisor that divides two or more numbers without remainder.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Finding the lowest common divisor (which is usually 1) instead of the highest.
educationalInfo.funFact: The product of HCF(a,b) and LCM(a,b) is always equal to a * b!
practiceQuestions: What is the HCF of 24 and 36?
Find the HCF of 17 and 23.

fraction.name: Fraction
fraction.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I represent a part of a whole.
I am written with one number above another, separated by a horizontal bar.
My top number is called the numerator.
My bottom number is called the denominator.
When cut a pizza into 4 equal slices and take 3, I am 3/4.

educationalInfo.definition: A number representing a part of a whole, expressed as one integer divided by another.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Adding fractions by adding numerators and denominators separately (e.g. 1/2 + 1/3 ≠ 2/5).
educationalInfo.funFact: Ancient Egyptians only used fractions with numerator 1 (unit fractions). They would write 3/4 as 1/2 + 1/4.
practiceQuestions: Simplify the fraction 12/16.
What is 2/3 + 1/4?

decimal.name: Decimal
decimal.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I am a way to write numbers that are not whole.
I use a point to separate whole parts from fractional parts.
Our counting system is Base 10, which is also my name.
The digit immediately to my right of the point represents tenths.
The number 3.14 is a famous example — it approximates pi.

educationalInfo.definition: A number expressed in the base-10 system using a decimal point to separate the whole from the fractional part.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Misaligning decimal points when adding or subtracting. Thinking 0.10 and 0.1 are different numbers.
educationalInfo.funFact: The decimal system was popularised in Europe by Simon Stevin in 1585, though India used it centuries earlier.
practiceQuestions: Convert 3/4 into a decimal.
What is 1.5 + 2.75?

percentage.name: Percentage
percentage.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I express a ratio out of 100.
My symbol looks like a slanted fraction sign.
50 of me is exactly one half.
I am used in discounts, tax, and exam scores.
To convert a fraction to me, multiply by 100.

educationalInfo.definition: A fraction whose denominator is 100, usually expressed using the % symbol.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing percentage increase with the final value. Forgetting to divide by 100 when converting.
educationalInfo.funFact: The % symbol evolved from the Italian 'per cento' (per hundred), eventually shortened to p/c, then rotated into %.
practiceQuestions: What is 35% of 200?
A shirt costs £40 with 20% off. What is the sale price?

integer.name: Integer
integer.subject: Arithmetic
clues: I am a complete number — no fractions, no decimals.
I include both positive and negative whole numbers.
I also include zero.
The set of me extends infinitely in both directions on a number line.

educationalInfo.definition: Any whole number (positive, negative, or zero) — not a fraction or decimal.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking negative numbers are not integers, or that fractions can be integers.
educationalInfo.funFact: The word integer comes from the Latin 'integer' meaning 'whole' or 'untouched'.
practiceQuestions: Is -7 an integer?
List three negative integers greater than -5.

right_triangle.name: Right Triangle
right_triangle.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a three-sided flat shape.
One of my interior angles measures exactly 90 degrees.
My longest side has a special name: the hypotenuse.
My area can be computed simply as half times base times height.
Trigonometry is built on my ratios.

educationalInfo.definition: A triangle in which one angle is a right angle (90 degrees).
examples: A triangle with angles 30, 60, and 90 degrees.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Assuming the right angle is always at the bottom-left corner.
educationalInfo.funFact: A right triangle can also be isosceles, with angles 45, 45, and 90 degrees.
practiceQuestions: Can a triangle have two right angles?
If a right triangle has an angle of 40 degrees, what is the third angle?

pythagoras_theorem.name: Pythagoras' Theorem
pythagoras_theorem.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a famous geometric principle.
I apply strictly to right-angled triangles.
I state that the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides.
My algebraic formula is written as a^2 + b^2 = c^2.
Numbers like (3, 4, 5) and (5, 12, 13) are my triples.

educationalInfo.definition: A fundamental relation in Euclidean geometry: a² + b² = c², where c is the hypotenuse of a right triangle.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Applying the formula to non-right triangles. Incorrectly squaring sides (e.g. thinking a + b = c).
educationalInfo.funFact: Ancient Babylonian, Indian, and Chinese mathematicians knew this relationship centuries before Pythagoras.
practiceQuestions: If the legs of a right triangle are 6 and 8, find the hypotenuse.
Is a triangle with side lengths 7, 24, and 25 a right triangle?

perimeter.name: Perimeter
perimeter.subject: Geometry
clues: I represent a boundary measurement.
I measure the total continuous distance around the outside of a 2D shape.
For a circle, my value is called the circumference.
For a polygon, you calculate me by adding all side lengths together.
For a square of side s, my formula is 4s.

educationalInfo.definition: The continuous line forming the boundary of a closed two-dimensional shape.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing perimeter with area (perimeter is linear length, not square units).
educationalInfo.funFact: The word perimeter comes from the Greek 'peri' (around) and 'metron' (measure).
practiceQuestions: Calculate the perimeter of a regular hexagon with side length 5cm.
What is the perimeter of a rectangle with length 10m and width 4m?

area.name: Area
area.subject: Geometry
clues: I measure two-dimensional space.
I count the amount of square units needed to cover a flat surface.
For a rectangle, my value is length multiplied by width.
For a circle, my formula is pi times radius squared.
My units are written with a superscript 2, such as square meters.

educationalInfo.definition: The quantity that expresses the extent of a two-dimensional figure in the plane.
examples: Area of a square with side 5cm = 25 cm².
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Measuring area in linear units instead of square units. Confusing perimeter and area formulas.
educationalInfo.funFact: A standard A4 sheet of paper has an area of exactly 1/16th of a square meter.
practiceQuestions: Find the area of a triangle with base 8cm and height 5cm.
If a square has a perimeter of 24cm, what is its area?

volume.name: Volume
volume.subject: Geometry
clues: I measure three-dimensional capacity.
I count the amount of cubic space occupied by a solid object.
For a cuboid, my calculation is length × width × height.
For a cylinder, my formula is pi × r² × h.
My units are written with a superscript 3, such as cm³ or cubic meters.

educationalInfo.definition: The amount of three-dimensional space enclosed by a closed surface.
examples: Volume of a cube with side 3cm = 27 cm³.
A cylinder with radius 2cm and height 5cm has volume ≈ 62.8 cm³.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Using area formulas instead of multiplying by height. Measuring volume in square units.
educationalInfo.funFact: One litre is defined as the volume of a cube that measures 10cm on each side (1000 cm³).
practiceQuestions: Calculate the volume of a box measuring 5cm × 4cm × 3cm.
What is the volume of a sphere of radius 3 units (in terms of pi)?

circle.name: Circle
circle.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a perfectly round shape.
Every point on my boundary is exactly the same distance from my centre.
That fixed distance is called my radius.
My boundary length is called my circumference, equal to 2πr.
The constant π (pi) is the ratio of my circumference to my diameter.

educationalInfo.definition: A round plane figure whose boundary consists of points equidistant from a fixed centre point.
examples: A coin is a circular shape.
The face of a clock is a circle with radius ≈ 10cm.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing radius with diameter (diameter = 2 × radius).
educationalInfo.funFact: A circle has the largest area of any 2D shape with a given perimeter — this is why bubbles are spherical.
practiceQuestions: Find the circumference of a circle with radius 7cm.
What is the area of a circle with diameter 10cm?

polygon.name: Polygon
polygon.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a closed flat shape made entirely of straight sides.
My name comes from Greek meaning 'many angles'.
Triangles, squares, and hexagons are all examples of me.
A regular version of me has all sides and angles equal.
The sum of my interior angles is (n-2) × 180 degrees, where n is my number of sides.

educationalInfo.definition: A plane figure with at least three straight sides and angles.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Including circles or shapes with curved sides as polygons.
educationalInfo.funFact: A polygon with a million sides (a megagon) looks indistinguishable from a circle to the naked eye.
practiceQuestions: What is the sum of interior angles of a pentagon?
What is a polygon with 8 sides called?

cylinder.name: Cylinder
cylinder.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a three-dimensional solid shape.
I have two circular faces that are parallel and identical.
A rectangular surface wraps around my sides.
My volume is calculated as pi times radius squared times height.
A tin can or a battery is a real-world example of me.

educationalInfo.definition: A three-dimensional solid with two parallel circular bases connected by a curved lateral surface.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Using the diameter instead of the radius in the volume formula.
educationalInfo.funFact: Cylinders are incredibly strong structures — the cylindrical shape of columns can hold enormous weight.
practiceQuestions: Find the volume of a cylinder with radius 3cm and height 10cm.
What is the surface area of a cylinder with r=5 and h=8?

coordinate.name: Coordinate
coordinate.subject: Geometry
clues: I am a set of numbers that describe a position.
I am used to locate a point on a grid or in space.
On a 2D plane, I come in ordered pairs like (x, y).
The origin of my system is at point (0, 0).
René Descartes invented the system I belong to, called the Cartesian system.

educationalInfo.definition: A pair (or set) of numbers used to indicate the position of a point on a graph or in space.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Swapping x and y values — always read (horizontal, vertical).
educationalInfo.funFact: Legend says Descartes invented his coordinate system while watching a fly on his ceiling, wondering how to describe its position.
practiceQuestions: Plot the point (4, -2) on a coordinate grid.
What coordinate is at the origin?

sphere.name: Sphere
sphere.subject: Geometry
clues: I am the 3D version of a circle.
Every point on my surface is equidistant from my centre.
My volume is calculated as (4/3) × pi × radius cubed.
A ball, globe, and bubble are all examples of me.
I have no edges, no vertices, and only one curved surface.

educationalInfo.definition: A perfectly round geometrical object in three-dimensional space that is the set of all points equidistant from a given centre.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing a sphere (solid) with a circle (flat 2D). Using area formula instead of volume formula.
educationalInfo.funFact: Of all 3D shapes, a sphere has the smallest surface area for a given volume — which is why planets are spherical.
practiceQuestions: Find the volume of a sphere with radius 6cm.
What is the surface area of a sphere (formula)?

variable.name: Variable
variable.subject: Algebra
clues: I am a basic building block of algebra.
I represent a quantity whose value is unknown or can change.
I am usually written as a letter from the alphabet.
The letters x, y, and z are my most common representatives.
In the term 5x, I am represented by the x.

educationalInfo.definition: A symbol (usually a letter) standing for an unknown numerical value in an equation or algebraic expression.
examples: In y = mx + c, x and y are variables.
In x + 2 = 5, x is the variable.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Treating variables as constants. Forgetting that different variables represent different values.
educationalInfo.funFact: René Descartes introduced the convention of using x, y, z for variables and a, b, c for constants.
practiceQuestions: Identify the variable in the expression 3a - 7.
Can a variable take multiple values in a function?

expression.name: Expression
expression.subject: Algebra
clues: I combine terms using math operations.
I contain numbers, variables, and operation symbols.
Unlike an equation, I do NOT contain an equals sign.
I can be simplified, but I cannot be solved on my own.
An example of my form is 3x + 2y - 7.

educationalInfo.definition: A mathematical phrase containing numbers, variables, and operators — but no equality sign.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing an expression with an equation (equations have '=', expressions do not).
educationalInfo.funFact: Expressions are the algebraic equivalent of a sentence fragment, while equations are complete sentences.
practiceQuestions: Simplify the expression: 2x + 3x - 4.
Is '5x + 3 = 13' an expression or an equation?

linear_equation.name: Linear Equation
linear_equation.subject: Algebra
clues: I am an algebraic statement of equality.
My graph forms a straight line.
The highest exponent of my variables is exactly 1.
My standard form is written as y = mx + c.
An example of my form is 2x + 5 = 11.

educationalInfo.definition: An equation between two variables that gives a straight line when plotted on a graph.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking equations with variables squared are linear. Forgetting to apply operations to both sides.
educationalInfo.funFact: Linear equations are the foundation of linear programming, used to optimize shipping and logistics.
practiceQuestions: Solve for x: 3x - 4 = 11.
Does the point (2, 5) lie on the line y = 2x + 1?

quadratic_equation.name: Quadratic Equation
quadratic_equation.subject: Algebra
clues: I am an algebraic relation.
My graph forms a U-shaped curve called a parabola.
The highest exponent of my variable is exactly 2.
My standard form is ax² + bx + c = 0.
My solutions can be found using factorisation, completing the square, or the quadratic formula.

educationalInfo.definition: Any equation that can be rearranged in standard form as ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Forgetting the ± sign when taking square roots. Missing the quadratic term when rearranging.
educationalInfo.funFact: The path of any thrown object — a basketball or a stone — is a parabola described by a quadratic equation.
practiceQuestions: Find the roots of x² - 9 = 0.
How many solutions does x² - 4x + 4 = 0 have?

inequality.name: Inequality
inequality.subject: Algebra
clues: I describe a comparison between two mathematical quantities.
I show that one value is larger or smaller than another.
I use symbols that resemble arrowheads or wedges.
When you multiply both sides by a negative number, you must flip my symbol.
Examples of my symbols are <, >, ≤, and ≥.

educationalInfo.definition: A mathematical statement indicating that one expression is greater than, less than, or not equal to another.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Forgetting to flip the inequality sign when dividing or multiplying by a negative number.
educationalInfo.funFact: The '<' and '>' symbols were introduced by mathematician Thomas Harriot in 1631.
practiceQuestions: Solve for x: -2x > 8.
Represent x ≥ 3 on a number line.

ratio.name: Ratio
ratio.subject: Algebra
clues: I compare two quantities of the same kind.
I show how much of one thing there is compared to another.
I am often written with a colon between two numbers.
When making orange juice, mixing 1 part concentrate with 3 parts water gives me as 1:3.
I can also be written as a fraction.

educationalInfo.definition: A relationship between two numbers indicating how many times the first contains the second.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Expressing ratio in wrong order. Not simplifying to lowest terms.
educationalInfo.funFact: The golden ratio (≈1.618) appears in the proportions of the Parthenon, sunflowers, and nautilus shells.
practiceQuestions: Simplify the ratio 15:25.
If a recipe needs flour and sugar in ratio 3:1, how much flour for 200g sugar?

sequence.name: Sequence
sequence.subject: Algebra
clues: I am an ordered list of numbers following a rule.
Each number in me is called a term.
I can be arithmetic (constant difference) or geometric (constant ratio).
Fibonacci created a famous version of me: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8...
If my terms increase by 3 each time starting from 2, I am 2, 5, 8, 11...

educationalInfo.definition: An ordered list of numbers (terms) following a defined rule or pattern.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing arithmetic sequences (addition) with geometric sequences (multiplication).
educationalInfo.funFact: The Fibonacci sequence (1,1,2,3,5,8...) appears in the spirals of sunflowers, pinecones, and galaxies.
practiceQuestions: Find the 7th term of the arithmetic sequence 4, 7, 10...
What type of sequence is 2, 4, 8, 16?

function.name: Function
function.subject: Algebra
clues: I am a special rule that maps each input to exactly one output.
I am often written using f(x) notation.
I can be pictured as a machine: input goes in, output comes out.
On a graph, I pass the vertical line test — any vertical line crosses me at most once.
f(x) = 2x + 3 means for any x, my output is double x plus 3.

educationalInfo.definition: A rule that assigns exactly one output value to each input value.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking a relation where one input gives multiple outputs is a function (it is not).
educationalInfo.funFact: Functions are the backbone of all computer programming — every program is ultimately a chain of functions.
practiceQuestions: If f(x) = 3x - 2, find f(4).
Is y = ±√x a function? Why or why not?

gradient.name: Gradient
gradient.subject: Algebra
clues: I measure the steepness of a line.
I am defined as rise divided by run.
A horizontal line has me equal to zero.
A vertical line has me as undefined.
In the equation y = mx + c, I am represented by the letter m.

educationalInfo.definition: The steepness or slope of a line, measured as the ratio of vertical change (rise) to horizontal change (run).
examples: A line through (0,0) and (2,4) has gradient 4/2 = 2.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Dividing run by rise instead of rise by run.
educationalInfo.funFact: Road gradients are expressed as percentages — a '10% gradient' means you rise 10m for every 100m you travel forward.
practiceQuestions: Find the gradient of the line through (1,2) and (3,6).
What is the gradient of y = -5x + 7?

matrix.name: Matrix
matrix.subject: Algebra
clues: I am a rectangular arrangement of numbers.
I organise my elements into rows and columns.
My size is described by rows × columns (e.g. a 2×3 has 2 rows and 3 columns).
Two of me can be multiplied only when the columns of the first equal the rows of the second.
I am used in computer graphics, machine learning, and physics simulations.

educationalInfo.definition: A rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns, used to represent linear transformations.
examples: An identity matrix has 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Trying to add matrices of different dimensions. Swapping rows and columns (that gives the transpose).
educationalInfo.funFact: Google's original PageRank algorithm was solved using a massive matrix operation over the entire web graph.
practiceQuestions: What is the order of a matrix with 3 rows and 2 columns?
Can you add a 2×3 matrix to a 3×2 matrix?

mean.name: Mean
mean.subject: Statistics
clues: I am a measure of central tendency.
People often call me the average.
To find me, you must sum all values in a dataset.
After summing, you divide by the total count of numbers.
For the dataset {2, 4, 9}, my value is 5.

educationalInfo.definition: The average value of a set of numbers, calculated by dividing the sum of the values by their count.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Forgetting to divide by the count of items. Getting skewed results from outliers.
educationalInfo.funFact: The arithmetic mean is just one type of mean; others include the geometric mean and harmonic mean.
practiceQuestions: Find the mean of {5, 8, 11, 16}.
If the sum of 5 numbers is 100, what is their mean?

median.name: Median
median.subject: Statistics
clues: I represent the middle point of a dataset.
Before finding me, you must sort the numbers in ascending order.
If the count is odd, I am exactly the middle number.
If the count is even, I am the mean of the two middle numbers.
For the sorted dataset {1, 3, 7, 10}, my value is 5.

educationalInfo.definition: The middle number in a sorted, ascending or descending list of numbers.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Forgetting to sort the numbers before picking the middle value.
educationalInfo.funFact: Unlike the mean, the median is robust to outliers — which is why median house prices are reported instead of average prices.
practiceQuestions: Find the median of {12, 3, 8, 15, 6}.
What is the median of {1, 2, 9, 10}?

mode.name: Mode
mode.subject: Statistics
clues: I am the most popular member of a dataset.
I measure the value that appears with the highest frequency.
A dataset can have one of me, multiple of me, or none at all.
I am the only average that can be used for descriptive words (categorical data).
For the dataset {2, 3, 3, 4, 5}, my value is 3.

educationalInfo.definition: The value that appears most frequently in a data set.
examples: Mode of {blue, red, blue, green} is blue.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking a dataset must have exactly one mode. Confusing frequency with the value itself.
educationalInfo.funFact: The word mode comes from the French 'la mode', meaning fashion — i.e., what is most popular.
practiceQuestions: What is the mode of {5, 7, 5, 8, 7, 5}?
Can a set of numbers have no mode?

range.name: Range
range.subject: Statistics
clues: I am the simplest measure of spread in a dataset.
I describe how spread out the data values are.
I am calculated by subtracting the smallest value from the largest.
A small value of me means the data is clustered closely together.
For the dataset {3, 7, 12, 20}, my value is 17.

educationalInfo.definition: The difference between the largest and smallest values in a dataset, measuring spread.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Subtracting the largest from the smallest (giving a negative result). Confusing range with mean.
educationalInfo.funFact: Meteorologists report daily temperature range as the difference between the day's highest and lowest temperatures.
practiceQuestions: Find the range of {8, 3, 15, 6, 11}.
If range is 12 and the minimum is 5, what is the maximum?

probability.name: Probability
probability.subject: Statistics
clues: I measure likelihood and chance.
My value always lies strictly between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
A value of 0 means impossible, while 1 means absolute certainty.
I am computed as successful outcomes divided by total outcomes.
The chance of rolling a 4 on a standard six-sided die is 1/6.

educationalInfo.definition: The branch of mathematics concerning numerical descriptions of how likely an event is to occur.
examples: Probability of heads on a coin flip = 0.5.
Drawing an ace from a deck = 4/52 = 1/13.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Expressing probability as a number greater than 1. Confusing odds (1 to 3) with probability (1/4).
educationalInfo.funFact: Modern probability theory was developed in the 17th century by Pascal and Fermat to solve gambling problems.
practiceQuestions: What is the probability of rolling an even number on a die?
If a bag has 3 red and 7 blue marbles, what is the probability of picking red?

venn_diagram.name: Venn Diagram
venn_diagram.subject: Statistics
clues: I am a visual way to show collections.
I represent mathematical sets and their relationships.
I consist of overlapping circles drawn inside a rectangular box.
The overlapping section represents the intersection of sets.
John Venn created me in 1880.

educationalInfo.definition: An illustration using overlapping circles to show relationships among groups of things.
examples: A diagram showing common multiples of 2 and 3 in the overlap.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Forgetting to include elements that belong outside both circles inside the universal set rectangle.
educationalInfo.funFact: The rectangular bounding box representing the universal set is mathematically required but often omitted in popular memes.
practiceQuestions: If set A has 5 items, B has 4, and they overlap by 2, what is |A ∪ B|?
What does the overlapping area in a Venn diagram represent?

set.name: Set
set.subject: Statistics
clues: I am a well-defined collection of distinct objects.
My members are called elements.
I am written using curly braces with elements separated by commas.
The size of me is called my cardinality.
The empty version of me is written as {} or the symbol ∅.

educationalInfo.definition: A well-defined collection of distinct objects, considered as an object in its own right.
examples: {1, 2, 3} is a set of three integers.
{a, e, i, o, u} is the set of vowels.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Allowing duplicate elements — a set can only contain each element once.
educationalInfo.funFact: Set theory, developed by Georg Cantor in the 1870s, is the foundation of all modern mathematics.
practiceQuestions: What is the union of {1,2,3} and {3,4,5}?
What is the intersection of {a,b,c} and {b,c,d}?

standard_deviation.name: Standard Deviation
standard_deviation.subject: Statistics
clues: I measure how spread out data is from the mean.
A small value of me means data points are clustered close to the average.
A large value of me means data is more dispersed.
I am the square root of the variance.
I am symbolised by the Greek letter sigma (σ).

educationalInfo.definition: A measure of the amount of variation or dispersion of a set of values from their mean.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing standard deviation with range. Forgetting to take the square root of variance.
educationalInfo.funFact: In a normal distribution, about 68% of data falls within 1 standard deviation of the mean, and 95% within 2.
practiceQuestions: If variance = 25, what is the standard deviation?
Which dataset has greater spread: {5,5,5} or {2,5,8}?

normal_distribution.name: Normal Distribution
normal_distribution.subject: Statistics
clues: I am a symmetric bell-shaped curve.
My peak sits exactly at the mean.
Most values cluster around my centre, with fewer values at the extremes.
I am completely described by just two values: mean and standard deviation.
Heights, IQ scores, and exam marks often follow my shape.

educationalInfo.definition: A probability distribution that is symmetric about the mean, forming a bell-shaped curve.
examples: Heights of adult males in a population.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Assuming all real-world data follows a normal distribution. Forgetting it is symmetric around the mean, not the median.
educationalInfo.funFact: Carl Friedrich Gauss studied this distribution so extensively it is also called the Gaussian distribution.
practiceQuestions: In a normal distribution, what percentage of data lies within 1 SD of the mean?
If mean = 50 and SD = 10, between what values does 95% of data fall?

scatter_graph.name: Scatter Graph
scatter_graph.subject: Statistics
clues: I am a type of statistical graph.
I plot pairs of values as individual dots.
I am used to investigate the relationship between two variables.
A line of best fit can be drawn through my points.
Positive correlation means my dots rise from left to right.

educationalInfo.definition: A graph using dots to represent values for two different variables, showing possible correlations.
examples: Plotting height vs weight for a class of students.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing correlation with causation — just because two things correlate does not mean one causes the other.
educationalInfo.funFact: Scatter graphs were first used by Francis Galton in the 1880s to study the relationship between parents' and children's heights.
practiceQuestions: What does a scatter graph with all points on a perfect line indicate?
Describe the correlation if dots fall from top-left to bottom-right.

sine.name: Sine
sine.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am a basic trigonometric function.
In a right triangle, I equal the length of the opposite side divided by the hypotenuse.
My value for a 30-degree angle is exactly 0.5.
My graph oscillates continuously between -1 and +1.
My mathematical abbreviation is sin.

educationalInfo.definition: The trigonometric function equal to opposite ÷ hypotenuse for an acute angle in a right triangle.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Dividing adjacent by hypotenuse instead of opposite by hypotenuse. Forgetting Degree mode on calculator.
educationalInfo.funFact: The word sine originates from a Latin mistranslation of the Arabic word 'jaib', meaning pocket or fold.
practiceQuestions: What is the value of sin(0)?
If opposite = 5 and hypotenuse = 10, find sin of the angle.

cosine.name: Cosine
cosine.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am a key trigonometric ratio.
In a right triangle, I equal the length of the adjacent side divided by the hypotenuse.
My value for a 60-degree angle is exactly 0.5.
At 0 degrees, my value is at its maximum of 1.
My mathematical abbreviation is cos.

educationalInfo.definition: The trigonometric function equal to adjacent ÷ hypotenuse for an acute angle in a right triangle.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Using the opposite side instead of the adjacent side in calculations.
educationalInfo.funFact: The cosine of an angle is identical to the sine of its complementary angle — hence the 'co-' prefix.
practiceQuestions: What is the value of cos(90°)?
If adjacent = 4 and hypotenuse = 5, find the cosine.

tangent.name: Tangent
tangent.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I represent a ratio of triangle legs.
In a right triangle, I equal the opposite side divided by the adjacent side.
I can also be calculated as sine divided by cosine.
My value for a 45-degree angle is exactly 1.
My mathematical abbreviation is tan.

educationalInfo.definition: A trigonometric function representing opposite ÷ adjacent in a right triangle.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Inverting the ratio (dividing adjacent by opposite gives cotangent, not tangent).
educationalInfo.funFact: At 90°, tangent is undefined because cosine is 0 — its graph shoots off to infinity.
practiceQuestions: What is tan(0°)?
Find tan if opposite = 8 and adjacent = 8.

hypotenuse.name: Hypotenuse
hypotenuse.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am a physical component of a right triangle.
I am always the longest side of the triangle.
I am always positioned directly opposite the 90-degree right angle.
In Pythagoras' formula a² + b² = c², I am represented by the letter c.
I serve as the denominator in both sine and cosine calculations.

educationalInfo.definition: The longest side of a right-angled triangle, opposite the right angle.
examples: In a 3-4-5 right triangle, the side of length 5 is the hypotenuse.
The diagonal of a rectangle acts as the hypotenuse.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Assuming the hypotenuse is the base or height. Applying the term to non-right-angle triangles.
educationalInfo.funFact: The word hypotenuse comes from the Greek 'hypoteinousa', meaning 'stretching under'.
practiceQuestions: In a right triangle with legs 5 and 12, what is the hypotenuse?
Which side is the hypotenuse in a triangle with angles 40°, 50°, 90°?

angle_of_elevation.name: Angle of Elevation
angle_of_elevation.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I measure an upwards line of sight.
I am formed between the horizontal ground and a target point above.
I look upwards, whereas my partner the angle of depression looks down.
I am equal to the angle of depression due to alternate interior angles.
You measure me when looking up at the top of a tree or building.

educationalInfo.definition: The angle formed by the line of sight and the horizontal plane for an object above the horizontal.
examples: Looking up at a bird in a tree.
Measuring the angle from the ground to the top of a flag pole.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Measuring the angle from the vertical instead of the horizontal ground.
educationalInfo.funFact: Surveyors use an instrument called a clinometer or theodolite to measure angles of elevation.
practiceQuestions: If you stand 10m away from a tree and look up at 45°, how tall is the tree?
Explain the difference between angle of elevation and depression.

vector.name: Vector
vector.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I have both magnitude and direction.
Unlike a scalar, I cannot be fully described by just a number.
I am drawn as an arrow.
In 2D, I am written as a column with x and y components.
Wind speed and velocity are real-world examples of me.

educationalInfo.definition: A quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction, represented as an arrow.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing vector with scalar. Adding vectors by adding only magnitudes (direction must be considered).
educationalInfo.funFact: GPS navigation constantly uses vector mathematics to calculate the direction and distance to your destination.
practiceQuestions: What is the resultant of vectors (3,4) and (-1,2)?
Give two examples of scalar quantities and two of vector quantities.

square_root.name: Square Root
square_root.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am the inverse operation of squaring.
I find the number that, when multiplied by itself, gives you the original number.
My symbol looks like a tick with a horizontal bar stretching over the number.
The square root of 144 is 12.
I can produce irrational numbers — for example, my value for 2 is approximately 1.414.

educationalInfo.definition: A value that, when multiplied by itself, gives the original number. Denoted by the √ symbol.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking √(a+b) = √a + √b (this is incorrect). Forgetting negative roots exist: both +3 and -3 square to 9.
educationalInfo.funFact: The ancient Greeks discovered that √2 is irrational — a result so shocking it reportedly caused a crisis in their mathematical philosophy.
practiceQuestions: What is √169?
Is √3 rational or irrational?

cube_root.name: Cube Root
cube_root.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am the inverse operation of cubing.
I find the number that multiplied by itself three times gives the original.
My symbol is the square root symbol with a small 3 in the notch.
The cube root of 27 is 3.
Unlike square roots, I can also produce negative results — the cube root of -8 is -2.

educationalInfo.definition: A value that when cubed (multiplied by itself three times) gives the original number. Denoted ∛.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Assuming cube roots always produce positive values (they can be negative for negative inputs).
educationalInfo.funFact: Finding cube roots was a major challenge for ancient mathematicians — no simple geometric method exists unlike for square roots.
practiceQuestions: What is ∛64?
Find ∛-125.

square.name: Square (Power)
square.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am the result of multiplying a number by itself.
I am also written using the exponent 2.
I am always non-negative for real numbers.
4 squared equals 16, and 10 squared equals 100.
My inverse operation is the square root.

educationalInfo.definition: A number multiplied by itself, equivalent to raising a number to the power of 2.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Thinking (-3)² = -9. (It equals +9 because negative × negative = positive.)
educationalInfo.funFact: The numbers 1, 4, 9, 16, 25... are called perfect squares because they are squares of whole numbers.
practiceQuestions: What is 12²?
Is 50 a perfect square?

cube.name: Cube (Power)
cube.subject: Trigonometry
clues: I am the result of multiplying a number by itself three times.
I am also written using the exponent 3.
The volume of a cube-shaped box is my practical application.
2 cubed equals 8, and 3 cubed equals 27.
My inverse operation is the cube root.

educationalInfo.definition: A number raised to the power of 3, equivalent to multiplying it by itself three times.
educationalInfo.commonMistakes: Confusing 3² (9) with 2³ (8). Forgetting that negative cubes stay negative.
educationalInfo.funFact: The numbers 1, 8, 27, 64, 125... are called perfect cubes. Ramanujan noticed that 1729 = 1³ + 12³ = 9³ + 10³.
practiceQuestions: What is 5³?
Calculate (-3)³.
